description: A consideration offered to justify a claim, a choice, or an action. A reason answers why something should be accepted, and it stands apart from a cause, a purpose, and a private motive, though any of those can also serve as a reason once someone actually offers it as one.
sound_symbolism: Rolling 're' begins in relation and immediately reaches beyond itself. Closed lips hold the stressed 'mo' in a deep rounded center. 'Tha' lets that sound out as breath and broad 'a'; the inward turn a reason takes before it is offered becomes audible.
grammatical_notes: A countable noun. Possessive order states what the reason supports: 'sherewa remotha' (a reason for the claim). As a clause participant: 'ha remotha ha sherewa panoru.' (This reason supports this claim.) Distinct from 'ka' (cause), 'porua' (purpose), 'thueli' (intend), and 'thesori' (evidence).

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Reasons remain considerations offered within conditions and perspectives, not permanent possessions that make a speaker infallible.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Speakers can request and examine reasons while keeping the person who offers them distinct from the claim under discussion.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A reason may arise from practical experience and communal memory as well as abstract analysis, provided its supporting role is made clear.

semantic_domains.cognition: a consideration that supports acceptance or action
semantic_domains.communication: a ground offered so others can examine a claim or choice
semantic_domains.wisdom: deliberative support whose relevance and limits can be considered
